question_text: Which of the following best describes the purpose of asking 'open-ended' questions in a conversation with a recruiter or client?

options.a: To test how much the other person knows about their own field.
options.b: To extend the conversation and signal engagement to the speaker.
options.c: To draw out detailed thoughts and context from the other person.
options.d: To guide the conversation toward your own prepared talking points.

question_text: When describing a project on your resume, which structure most effectively communicates business value?

options.a: Project supervisor named, team size, duration of work.
options.b: Problem addressed, your contribution, and measurable outcome.
options.c: Personal effort invested, time spent, final deliverable produced.
options.d: Technology stack used, features built, modules completed.

question_text: In professional contexts, the acronym BANT refers to a framework for qualifying opportunities. What does BANT stand for?

options.a: Brand, Audience, Network, Timing.
options.b: Benefit, Approach, Negotiation, Trust.
options.c: Budget, Authority, Need, Timeline.
options.d: Business, Analytics, Numbers, Targets.

question_text: An 'elevator pitch' should ideally be:

options.a: Brief and specific enough to spark interest.
options.b: Comprehensive across your full background.
options.c: Polished and rehearsed for any context.
options.d: Formal enough to suit senior listeners.

question_text: In persuasive communication, what is the distinction between a 'feature' and a 'benefit'?

options.a: Feature is internal; benefit is what marketing emphasises.
options.b: Feature is technical; benefit is its financial value to them.
options.c: Feature is what it is; benefit is what it does for them.
options.d: Feature is standard; benefit is a customisation for them.

question_text: The term 'BATNA' in negotiation refers to:

options.a: The range within which both parties can agree.
options.b: Your initial offer that anchors the conversation.
options.c: Your best alternative if no agreement is reached.
options.d: The bottom line you are unwilling to negotiate past.

question_text: Before a job interview at a specific company, the MOST valuable preparation activity is:

options.a: Reading widely about the industry and recent trends.
options.b: Rehearsing answers to commonly asked interview questions.
options.c: Reviewing your resume thoroughly to recall key details.
options.d: Studying the company's recent work, news, and people.

question_text: The STAR framework for answering behavioural interview questions stands for:

options.a: Situation, Task, Action, Result.
options.b: Story, Theme, Approach, Reflection.
options.c: Skills, Tasks, Achievements, Roles.
options.d: Strategy, Tactics, Activities, Returns.

question_text: Which sequence best represents a typical sales conversation cycle?

options.a: Send proposal, schedule meeting, negotiate terms, sign deal.
options.b: Discover need, present fit, handle concerns, agree next step.
options.c: Present offering, handle questions, close deal, deliver work.
options.d: Build rapport, discuss budget, present pricing, follow up.

question_text: Why is understanding the 'customer journey' important even if you are not in a sales role?

options.a: All modern companies expect employees to think like marketers.
options.b: Sales and marketing perform better when others understand their work.
options.c: Most professional work indirectly affects a customer's experience.
options.d: Customer journey awareness is part of standard business education.

question_text: The most accurate definition of a 'personal brand' for a fresh graduate is:

options.a: The polish of your resume, LinkedIn, and other online presence.
options.b: The credentials and titles you have earned in your career so far.
options.c: The reputation that follows you across professional contexts.
options.d: The professional network you have built across various roles.

question_text: Which of the following is the most accurate definition of 'active listening'?

options.a: Taking detailed notes while another person is talking.
options.b: Staying silent while another person is speaking aloud.
options.c: Maintaining steady eye contact during the conversation.
options.d: Fully engaging with the speaker to truly understand them.

question_text: A 'value proposition' is most precisely defined as:

options.a: The benefits that differentiate you from your competitors.
options.b: The specific value you offer to a specific customer type.
options.c: The list of features your product or service provides.
options.d: The price-quality position you take in your market.

question_text: In interview or sales contexts, the most professionally effective response to a tough objection is:

options.a: Accept the objection and concede the point gracefully.
options.b: Defend your position firmly with the strongest evidence.
options.c: Acknowledge the concern, ask to understand, then respond.
options.d: Redirect to a stronger point you would prefer to make.

question_text: It is the first round of a campus placement. The HR manager opens with: "In one minute, why should we consider you for this role?" Which approach is MOST effective?

options.a: Ask the HR manager what they are looking for in a candidate before answering.
options.b: Confidently state your CGPA, college, graduation year, and top three technical skills in detail.
options.c: Mention you are hardworking, dedicated, quick to learn, and willing to give your best.
options.d: Connect a specific project or internship to a concrete contribution for their role.

question_text: A senior at your internship asks: "Can you build us a simple dashboard?" You have a few questions but want to seem proactive. What is the BEST approach?

options.a: Schedule 15 minutes: ask who uses it, what decisions to support, what data exists.
options.b: Start building based on your best guess of what they need; adjust on feedback.
options.c: Ask one clarifying question by email and proceed once you get a reply back.
options.d: Send them a list of 20 detailed technical questions before starting any work.

question_text: An interviewer comments: "You have no real work experience." How do you respond most effectively?

options.a: "Most freshers don't have experience — I am no different from any of them."
options.b: "My internship was a real working environment, so I would consider that experience."
options.c: "True, but my final-year project gave me applied experience solving real problems."
options.d: "You are correct — I am a fresher, but I am willing to learn and work hard."

question_text: You are demonstrating a class project to evaluators. Mid-demo, a feature crashes unexpectedly. What do you do?

options.a: Acknowledge the issue briefly, explain what the feature does, and continue the demo.
options.b: Stop the demo entirely and offer to schedule a follow-up session at a later date.
options.c: Apologise repeatedly and ask if you can restart the entire demo from the beginning.
options.d: Quickly switch to showing the source code instead of the running application live.

question_text: You receive a job offer. The package is 20% below what you were expecting based on similar offers from peers. What is the most effective initial response?

options.a: Accept the offer to avoid losing it, then renegotiate after you start working.
options.b: Mention casually that other companies are offering more, hoping they improve the offer.
options.c: Reject the offer and ask for the higher number you were originally expecting.
options.d: Express appreciation, ask for time to review, then counter with evidence-based numbers.

question_text: You have 3 minutes to pitch your final-year project to industry judges. Most of them are senior managers, not technical experts. The MOST effective approach is to:

options.a: Spend 2 minutes explaining the technical architecture and 1 minute on the final results.
options.b: Lead with the technologies you used to demonstrate your technical depth and skill.
options.c: Walk through the project chronologically — what you did week by week from the start.
options.d: Open with the real-world problem, cover what you built, end with results and next steps.

question_text: Your internship manager asks for a status update on a task you are 60% done with but are stuck on a technical issue. The BEST response is:

options.a: "60% done. Stuck on X — tried A and B. Can we discuss for 10 minutes today?"
options.b: "Going well, will update you with more details soon when there's more progress."
options.c: "It is taking longer than expected — there are some technical issues to work through."
options.d: "I will finish by end of week — I am still working through some implementation issues."

question_text: You are at the end of a final-round interview. The interviewer asks: "Do you have any questions for us?" The MOST effective question to ask is:

options.a: "How many days of leave do employees get per year and what is the holiday policy?"
options.b: "What is the company culture like and how would you describe the work environment?"
options.c: "What is the salary range for this position and when will I hear back from you?"
options.d: "What does success in this role look like in the first six months of joining?"

question_text: In a group discussion for a placement, the conversation has gone off-topic for 5 minutes. You want to bring it back without sounding dismissive. The most effective move is:

options.a: Wait silently — eventually the discussion will return to the original topic on its own.
options.b: Raise the volume of your voice gradually to redirect the group's attention back.
options.c: Interject firmly with: "Let's get back to the actual topic the panel has set for us."
options.d: Acknowledge a point made, then bridge back to the main question building on that.

question_text: On your resume, which of the following is the MOST compelling project description?

options.a: "Developed a fully functional, scalable, secure web application from scratch and tested."
options.b: "Worked on a team project involving several technologies that was deployed successfully."
options.c: "Built a web application using React, Node.js, MongoDB, and Docker for deployment."
options.d: "Built a queue app for 80+ patients/day, cutting wait time from 45 to 18 minutes."

question_text: An interviewer asks: "Five other candidates have similar qualifications. Why you?" The strongest response is:

options.a: Mention you are dedicated, hardworking, and a quick learner — universal positive traits.
options.b: Express enthusiasm for the company and willingness to grow with them over the years.
options.c: List your top five achievements from college and let the interviewer decide for themself.
options.d: Connect 1–2 of your strengths directly to this role's specific challenges and needs.

question_text: Two days after your final interview, you have not heard back. The recruiter said "by end of this week". What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.a: Call the recruiter every day until you hear back about the decision on your application.
options.b: Send a polite, brief follow-up email reiterating your interest and asking for an update.
options.c: Post on social media that you are anxiously waiting for a response to your interview.
options.d: Wait silently — they will reach out when they are ready with their final decision.

question_text: You are building your LinkedIn profile as a fresher. Which approach will best support your job search?

options.a: Headline your target role, a short summary with achievements, and feature 2–3 projects.
options.b: Keep your profile minimal and let recruiters discover you through your online activity.
options.c: List every skill, course, and certificate you have ever earned to appear well-rounded.
options.d: Copy the profile structure of someone successful in your target field very closely.

question_text: You are freelancing on the side to build experience. A client asks for a 40% discount, citing budget constraints. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Refuse the discount firmly and walk away from the conversation with the client.
options.b: Offer the discount but require the full upfront payment as a counter-condition.
options.c: Offer a reduced scope at a lower price, preserving your rate per unit of work done.
options.d: Agree to the discount to secure the project — you need the experience for your resume.

question_text: I feel confident pitching myself or my work to people in positions of authority.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When someone is speaking, I find it easy to listen fully without thinking about my reply.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable asking clarifying questions to senior people, even when others stay silent.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: Before recommending a solution, I instinctively ask questions to understand the real problem.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can tell a clear, structured story about my work that captures attention and conveys impact.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: After a rejection (interview, application, pitch), I can analyse and move forward without losing motivation.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can explain the value of my work in business terms (not just technical terms) when needed.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable advocating for my own work and accomplishments when appropriate.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I feel uncomfortable when I have to 'sell' myself in interviews or networking situations.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I often downplay my achievements when describing my work to others.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you proactively reach out to professionals (alumni, seniors, industry contacts) to ask thoughtful questions about their work?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Before an important presentation or interview, how often do you rehearse out loud (not just review your notes mentally)? PILLAR 02  ·  LEADERSHIP & INFLUENCE Self-leadership, accountability, growth mindset, and the lateral influence skills that distinguish strong individual contributors from average ones. Items in this pillar: 40

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Which of the following best distinguishes leadership from management?

options.a: Leadership sets direction; management organises execution.
options.b: Leadership is about people; management is about processes.
options.c: Leadership happens at the top; management happens below.
options.d: Leadership requires charisma; management requires discipline.

question_text: A 'growth mindset' (as defined by Carol Dweck) is:

options.a: Belief that ability is built through effort and strategy.
options.b: Belief that effort matters more than results in development.
options.c: Belief that some people have natural talent that others lack.
options.d: Belief that positive thinking drives all professional success.

question_text: Workplace 'accountability' is most accurately defined as:

options.a: Following all instructions given by your direct manager.
options.b: Reporting honestly on what others have or haven't done.
options.c: Taking the blame when something goes wrong on a project.
options.d: Owning your commitments and the outcomes they produce.

question_text: The Eisenhower Matrix categorises tasks along which two dimensions?

options.a: Urgency and importance.
options.b: Difficulty and duration.
options.c: Cost and complexity.
options.d: Effort and visibility.

question_text: When receiving difficult feedback, the MOST useful first response is:

options.a: Explain the context that led to the behaviour discussed.
options.b: Defend yourself if the feedback feels unfair or off.
options.c: Listen fully, ask clarifying questions, then reflect.
options.d: Thank the giver and move on to avoid awkwardness.

question_text: 'Psychological safety' in a team, as researched by Amy Edmondson, refers to:

options.a: Members feeling protected from criticism by senior leaders.
options.b: Members feeling physically and emotionally safe in the office.
options.c: Members feeling safe to take interpersonal risks at work.
options.d: Members agreeing on most decisions before moving forward.

question_text: Which of the following best demonstrates 'initiative' in a workplace context?

options.a: Acting on a useful idea before being asked to.
options.b: Suggesting many ideas in team meetings each week.
options.c: Volunteering for any opportunity that becomes available.
options.d: Doing work outside your formal job description.

question_text: What does 'leading without authority' typically refer to?

options.a: Influencing others when you have no formal power over them.
options.b: Persuading others using charisma rather than positional power.
options.c: Leading effectively despite the absence of senior support.
options.d: Building informal authority before you have a leadership title.

question_text: You discover a significant error in code you wrote yesterday that has caused a bug in your team's shared codebase. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.a: Mention it casually in the next stand-up meeting as a minor issue to be aware of.
options.b: Immediately inform your team lead, explain what happened, propose a fix, and follow through.
options.c: Wait to see if anyone notices the bug, then decide whether to address it openly.
options.d: Quietly fix the bug without telling anyone — it was your mistake so you should resolve it.

question_text: Two members of your project team are not speaking after a disagreement, and it is affecting the project. You are a peer. The most effective action is:

options.a: Speak with each privately, then suggest a joint conversation focused on the project.
options.b: Stay completely neutral and avoid both of them until they resolve the conflict themselves.
options.c: Report the conflict to the professor or manager immediately for them to address.
options.d: Take the side of whichever team member you are closer to and isolate the other one.

question_text: Your manager has asked the team to implement an approach that you believe will create technical debt. What is the most professional response?

options.a: Request a private chat, share your concern with reasoning, then commit to the decision.
options.b: Refuse to implement it and explain your reasoning to the rest of the team openly.
options.c: Implement it but document your disagreement in writing in case something goes wrong.
options.d: Implement it silently as instructed — they are senior and know what is best here.

question_text: Your manager offers you a project that uses a technology you have never used before, with a tight timeline. What is the most professionally appropriate response?

options.a: Accept enthusiastically without raising any concerns about the timeline or learning curve.
options.b: Accept, ask clarifying questions on scope, outline a learning plan, flag support needs.
options.c: Decline politely and ask for a project that matches your current set of skills better.
options.d: Ask if a more experienced colleague can take it instead of giving it to you.

question_text: On a 5-person student project team you are leading, one member is consistently not contributing. The deadline is in two weeks. What is the BEST first action?

options.a: Confront them in the next team meeting so the issue is openly addressed by the group.
options.b: Remove them from the team immediately and redistribute their work to the other members.
options.c: Speak privately: ask if everything is okay, share specific impact, agree on what changes.
options.d: Take on their work yourself to ensure the project succeeds before the deadline.

question_text: You have three urgent tasks and only enough time to do two well. Which approach reflects the strongest self-leadership?

options.a: Pick the two easiest to complete to maximise the number of tasks marked as done.
options.b: Assess highest impact, defer or delegate the rest, communicate trade-off to your manager.
options.c: Work overtime to attempt all three to full quality regardless of how long it takes.
options.d: Try to do all three quickly and accept that the quality will suffer across the board.

question_text: A more junior teammate asks for help on a topic you know well. You are mid-task and slightly behind on your own deadline. The best response is:

options.a: Stop your work entirely and walk them through the entire topic from start to finish.
options.b: Send them a link to the documentation and continue your task without further engagement.
options.c: Tell them you are too busy right now and they should figure it out for themselves.
options.d: Take 5–10 minutes to point them in the right direction, then return to your own work.

question_text: Your manager publicly praises a team result that was primarily driven by a junior colleague's contribution, but credits you instead. The most professionally appropriate response is:

options.a: Redirect in the moment: "Thank you — that was driven by [colleague]; she deserves it."
options.b: Stay silent in the moment, then mention the issue to HR after the meeting ends.
options.c: Privately tell the colleague that you appreciate their work on the team's recent project.
options.d: Accept the credit graciously — recognition is rare in workplaces anyway, so take it.

question_text: You have been rejected from 7 job applications in the past month. What is the most constructive next step?

options.a: Lower your standards and apply to roles below your skill level to secure something soon.
options.b: Pause for review: applying to right roles, resume positioning, interview performance.
options.c: Step away from job searching for a month to recover emotionally before trying again.
options.d: Send 50 more applications immediately — job searching is fundamentally a numbers game.

question_text: In a code review, a senior engineer publicly identifies a serious flaw in your work. What is the MOST professionally mature response in the moment?

options.a: Apologise repeatedly for the mistake and offer to redo the entire feature again.
options.b: Defend your design choice and explain the constraints you were under when writing it.
options.c: Stay silent until the meeting ends, then process the feedback privately on your own.
options.d: Acknowledge openly: "You're right — that's a real problem. Let me think about a fix."

question_text: You notice that your team uses an inefficient manual process every week. The MOST appropriate way to take initiative is:

options.a: Continue with the existing process — improving it is not part of your job description.
options.b: Build a tool to automate it in your free time and present the finished version to your team.
options.c: Mention to your manager that the process seems inefficient and wait for direction.
options.d: Propose to manager: here's the inefficiency, here's the fix, want me to prototype it?

question_text: You are deeply stuck on a technical problem and have spent 4 hours without progress. The MOST effective next step is:

options.a: Continue trying for another 4 hours by yourself — persistence is essential to solving it.
options.b: Wait until tomorrow to ask anyone for help — your colleagues are all busy today.
options.c: Document what you tried, articulate where you are stuck, ask a colleague for 15 minutes.
options.d: Ask the team in a group chat without first articulating what you have already tried.

question_text: Your manager asks you to take on additional work that would extend your day to 12 hours for the next two weeks. You believe your current scope is already at capacity. The MOST professional response is:

options.a: Agree to the additional work without any comment to demonstrate your commitment fully.
options.b: Agree but secretly defer or skip parts of your existing work to make room for the new.
options.c: Refuse the additional work outright — work-life balance is essential to your wellbeing.
options.d: Acknowledge, share workload, ask what to deprioritise, propose how to accommodate it.

question_text: You and a teammate had a sharp disagreement two days ago and have barely spoken since. You both have to collaborate this week. What is the BEST first step?

options.a: Pretend the disagreement did not happen at all and just move on with the project work.
options.b: Approach privately: "I've been thinking about our talk. I value working with you."
options.c: Wait for them to be the one who brings the disagreement up first in conversation.
options.d: Address it openly in the next team meeting in front of the rest of the group.

question_text: On the first day of leading a 4-person group project, what is the most effective use of your time?

options.a: Run 30 min meeting: strengths and interests, agree on roles, set comms, align on first.
options.b: Send a detailed plan document to everyone via email and ask for confirmation from them.
options.c: Start assigning tasks to each team member immediately to demonstrate strong decisiveness.
options.d: Take on the most complex work yourself and assign the rest to the other team members.

question_text: As team lead on a college project, you have a complex sub-task. The right approach to delegating it is:

options.a: Assign it to whoever has the most free time on the team this week, regardless of skill.
options.b: Match it to a teammate whose strengths fit, brief outcome clearly, support without micro.
options.c: Hand it off with detailed step-by-step instructions for exactly how to do every part.
options.d: Do it yourself — only you can really ensure the level of quality the team needs here.

question_text: I see challenges as opportunities to grow rather than threats to my identity or competence.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When something goes wrong in my work, I focus on what I can do differently rather than what others should have done.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I act on opportunities to improve things in my environment without waiting to be asked.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I regularly reflect on my actions and outcomes to identify what I can do better.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am able to bring others around to my point of view when I genuinely believe in it.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable making decisions even when I do not have complete information.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I enjoy helping others learn and develop, even when it does not directly benefit me.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I have a clear sense of the kind of professional I want to become in the next 3–5 years.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am willing to take an unpopular position when I believe it is the right thing to do.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I manage my time and priorities effectively without external pressure.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When tasks become difficult, I tend to wait for someone else to take charge.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When my work has a poor outcome, I usually find the cause is something outside my control.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you write down your goals for the week and review them at the end of the week?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you read or learn from non-required sources (books, courses, podcasts) related to your career growth?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you proactively ask for feedback on your work — not in a formal review, but in normal interactions?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you follow through on commitments to others without needing reminders? PILLAR 03  ·  PERSONALITY & COMMUNICATION Spoken and written communication, emotional intelligence, listening, assertiveness, and the interpersonal skills that determine how others experience working with you. Items in this pillar: 50

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: According to Albert Mehrabian's much-cited research on communication of feelings and attitudes, what is the often-quoted breakdown of message impact?
Which element is MOST important in a professional email to maximise the chance it gets read and acted on?

options.a: A polished closing that reflects strong professionalism.
options.b: A detailed body that includes all relevant background.
options.c: A formal greeting that acknowledges seniority and role.
options.d: A specific subject line that signals the main ask.

question_text: Assertive communication is BEST described as:

options.a: Speaking only when you are certain your view is correct.
options.b: Stating your view forcefully to ensure you are clearly heard.
options.c: Stating your view clearly while respecting the other person.
options.d: Saving disagreement for private one-on-one conversations.

question_text: Cognitive empathy is distinguished from emotional empathy by:

options.a: Cognitive empathy is conscious; emotional empathy is automatic.
options.b: Cognitive empathy is rare; emotional empathy is common.
options.c: Cognitive empathy understands; emotional empathy feels.
options.d: Cognitive empathy is learned; emotional empathy is innate.

question_text: The 'inverted pyramid' structure used in journalism and business writing means:

options.a: Lead with the most important point, then add detail.
options.b: Build up context first, then deliver the main conclusion.
options.c: Cover all viewpoints before stating your position.
options.d: Open with a story, then move to the central point.

question_text: 'Cultural intelligence' (CQ) refers to:

options.a: Sensitivity to cultural differences in your own home country.
options.b: Deep familiarity with the etiquette of multiple cultures.
options.c: The ability to speak several languages with reasonable fluency.
options.d: The capability to function effectively across cultures.

question_text: You need to email your manager to ask for time off for an important personal matter next month. The MOST effective email is:

options.a: Subject: "Leave". Body: "Hi, can I take leave on March 15-17 next month? Thanks."
options.b: Subject: "Important — Personal Time Off Needed". Body: A long detailed explanation.
options.c: Clear subject and request: dates, reason in one line, work coverage, asks for approval.
options.d: Subject: "Hi". Body: "Hope you are well. Could I take some time off next month please?"

question_text: A close friend on your team produces work that is below the team's standards. You are uncomfortable raising it. The most professionally mature action is:

options.a: Speak privately, lead with respect, share specific observations, focus on impact not blame.
options.b: Stay silent — the team lead will eventually notice and address it through proper channels.
options.c: Hint at the issue through humour in team conversations to make the point a bit gently.
options.d: Discuss the issue with another colleague to validate your concern before raising it openly.

question_text: In a team meeting, a colleague makes a factual error about a project you worked on. The most effective response is:

options.a: Immediately correct them publicly in the meeting so others have the right information.
options.b: Ignore the error completely since it does not significantly affect the overall outcome.
options.c: Stay silent in the meeting and correct them privately later after the session ends.
options.d: Add gently: "I worked on that — let me share what we actually found in the analysis."

question_text: A colleague is sharing a personal frustration about a project setback. The MOST supportive response is:

options.a: Share your own similar story to help them feel less alone in their current situation.
options.b: Immediately offer solutions and suggestions for what they should do next about the issue.
options.c: Tell them not to worry about it — these things usually have a way of working out fine.
options.d: Listen fully, acknowledge how they feel, and ask what kind of support they would like.

question_text: During your group's presentation to senior management, your co-presenter freezes and forgets their next point. The most professionally appropriate response is:

options.a: Wait silently for them to recover from the freeze on their own without any prompting.
options.b: Take over the presentation and continue seamlessly without acknowledging the awkward moment.
options.c: Bridge smoothly: "Let me build on that point..." giving them a moment to recover.
options.d: Apologise to the audience for the awkward pause that has occurred during the talk.

question_text: You are 4 weeks into a 6-week project and have hit a significant unforeseen technical blocker that will delay delivery by 1 week. The MOST appropriate action with stakeholders is:

options.a: Wait until you have a clear solution before communicating, to avoid alarming the stakeholders.
options.b: Inform your manager only and let them decide whether to inform the wider stakeholders.
options.c: Mention the delay casually in the next routine update meeting with the team next week.
options.d: Communicate now: blocker, what you're doing, expected delay, what you need from them.

question_text: Your team includes members from different countries. In your first team meeting, you notice some members are quieter than others. The MOST inclusive response is:

options.a: Specifically call on the quieter members by name to share their views with the team.
options.b: Assume the cultural difference means they prefer not to participate in the discussion.
options.c: Continue the discussion as is — the quieter members will speak when they have something.
options.d: Invite explicitly: "I'd love to hear other views — [Name], what's your take on this?"

question_text: You are interviewing on video call. Which of the following choices best supports a confident, professional impression?

options.a: Lean slightly back during the call to convey a sense of relaxed and easy confidence.
options.b: Look at the interviewer's face on screen continuously to maintain perceived eye contact.
options.c: Sit very still throughout the entire conversation to avoid appearing distracted to them.
options.d: Look into the camera when speaking, sit upright, and use natural facial expressions.

question_text: Your manager wants the team to adopt a specific solution. You strongly believe a different approach is better. The most effective way to disagree is:

options.a: Request a 1-on-1: "I'd like to share a different angle — happy to be wrong on it."
options.b: Implement your manager's solution but signal your disagreement quietly to your teammates.
options.c: Send a written document outlining both options for your manager to consider in detail.
options.d: State your disagreement firmly in the team meeting where everyone else is present too.

question_text: Your manager publicly praises a result that came from your work. The most effective response is:

options.a: Change the subject quickly to something else to avoid drawing further attention to it.
options.b: Modestly deflect with: "Oh, it was nothing really — anyone could have done it."
options.c: Accept simply: "Thank you" — and name teammates who contributed if applicable.
options.d: Highlight further details of your specific contribution to ensure full credit goes to you.

question_text: An interviewer asks: "Tell me about a time you failed at something." The MOST effective story has which structure?

options.a: Describe situation, what went wrong, what you did to address it, lesson applied after.
options.b: Describe a failure that was caused entirely by external factors beyond your own control.
options.c: Describe a "failure" that was actually a hidden strength ("I work too hard sometimes").
options.d: Pick a small, low-stakes failure to minimise the risk it creates in the interview.

question_text: Your manager tells you in a 1-on-1: "Your work is technically good, but you tend to dominate team discussions and others have raised it." The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Listen, ask for an example to be sure you understand, thank them, commit to adjusting.
options.b: Apologise profusely for the issue and promise to be much quieter in all future meetings.
options.c: Defend yourself by explaining that you only speak when you have valuable input to share.
options.d: Ask who specifically raised the concern so you can address it with them directly later.

question_text: A teammate shows you a presentation they have prepared and asks for your feedback. You think it has significant issues. The MOST useful response is:

options.a: Tell them it has many problems and that they should probably rework most of it now.
options.b: Tell them it looks great to avoid hurting their confidence before the presentation day.
options.c: Offer to redo the presentation yourself to save them time and ensure it is good quality.
options.d: Start with what works, then share 2–3 specific suggestions framed as strengtheners.

question_text: You disagree with a senior colleague's posted opinion on LinkedIn. The most professionally appropriate response is:

options.a: Comment publicly with your counter-argument to maintain your visible thought leadership.
options.b: Share the post on your own profile with critical commentary added to express your view.
options.c: Ignore it completely and move on without engaging with the post in any way at all.
options.d: Engage privately if at all — public disagreement on social media rarely helps your career.

question_text: You receive an email from a senior person that feels curt and possibly rude. The MOST professional first interpretation is:

options.a: Assume they are busy and reading tone over email is risky — reply to the content.
options.b: Forward the email to a friend for a second opinion on whether they were being rude.
options.c: Assume they are being deliberately dismissive and respond cautiously to their email.
options.d: Respond with a similarly curt reply to match the energy of their original email message.

question_text: You want to reach out to a senior professional in your target field on LinkedIn for advice. The MOST effective opening message is:

options.a: A generic message asking for some general career advice they might be willing to share.
options.b: A simple connection request with no message attached to it at all from your side.
options.c: A brief, specific message: reference their work, one focused question, respect their time.
options.d: A long detailed message about your career goals and a request for them to mentor you.

question_text: In a small-group discussion, after you finish making a point, no one responds for several seconds. The most professionally composed response is:

options.a: Repeat your point in different words to fill the silence and re-engage the listeners.
options.b: Wait quietly — silence often means people are thinking; jumping in dilutes your point.
options.c: Quickly ask a clarifying question to invite the rest of the group to respond to you.
options.d: Apologise with "Sorry, maybe I wasn't clear..." to soften the awkward silence.

question_text: You are at a professional networking event for the first time. You feel awkward and don't know anyone. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Approach the most senior person present and introduce yourself to them directly first.
options.b: Stand by the food table — other attendees will eventually come over to you to talk.
options.c: Approach individuals or small groups, open with a question about the event, listen well.
options.d: Leave the event early — networking events are not productive for early-career people.

question_text: How often do you read books, long articles, or substantial content beyond required academic material?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Before a presentation or important conversation, how often do you specifically plan how you will open?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you re-read your own emails or messages before sending them?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you reflect after a significant conversation or meeting on what you could have communicated better?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: When in conversation, how often do you maintain natural eye contact (without forcing it)?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you pause briefly before responding in important conversations (rather than answering immediately)?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you take notes during important meetings or learning conversations?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: When in conversation with someone new, how often do you ask them follow-up questions rather than talking about yourself? PILLAR 04  ·  MENTAL WELLNESS & RESILIENCE Self-awareness, emotional regulation, recovery from setbacks, and the foundational wellness habits that determine sustainable professional performance. Items in this pillar: 35

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)

question_text: The 'fight-flight-freeze' response is part of which physiological system?

options.a: Sympathetic nervous system.
options.b: Limbic emotional system.
options.c: Cardiovascular system.
options.d: Parasympathetic nervous system.

question_text: What does the bulk of contemporary sleep research suggest about adult sleep needs?

options.a: Most adults can adapt to 5–6 hours without measurable impact.
options.b: Optimal sleep need varies widely among healthy individuals.
options.c: Most adults need 7–9 hours for sustained performance.
options.d: Sleep quality matters far more than total quantity of sleep.

question_text: The World Health Organization recognises burnout as:

options.a: An occupational phenomenon, not a medical condition.
options.b: A diagnosable mental health condition requiring treatment.
options.c: A subtype of anxiety disorder in workplace contexts.
options.d: A chronic stress reaction with no formal status.

question_text: 'Catastrophising' is a cognitive distortion in which:

options.a: You assume the worst possible outcome is the likely one.
options.b: You believe negative emotions reflect objective realities.
options.c: You exaggerate the negative aspects of a current situation.
options.d: You focus on a single negative point and ignore others.

question_text: You have three urgent assignments due in 48 hours, and you are feeling overwhelmed. What is the MOST constructive immediate action?

options.a: Push through with caffeine and skip sleep entirely to complete all of them on time.
options.b: List all three with time estimates, identify what's doable well, accept what is not.
options.c: Ask for extensions on all three assignments simultaneously to give yourself more time.
options.d: Complete the easiest task first to build momentum before tackling the harder ones.

question_text: You failed an important exam despite preparation. The MOST psychologically healthy response is:

options.a: Increase study hours dramatically to compensate for the gap that the failure exposed.
options.b: Feel disappointment briefly, then review what went wrong, and adjust your approach.
options.c: Tell yourself failure is normal and move on without any reflection on what happened.
options.d: Conclude that you are not really cut out for this field after this difficult setback.

question_text: You have been working on a project submission for hours and feel you cannot submit because it is not perfect. The deadline is in 30 minutes. What is the MOST psychologically healthy action?

options.a: Recognise that "done and good" beats "never finished and perfect" — submit what is done.
options.b: Continue working until the very last moment of the deadline and submit whatever state it is in.
options.c: Email the professor to request an extension so you can do the project properly later.
options.d: Submit it but include a long apology in the email for its remaining imperfections.

question_text: A close friend is going through a difficult time (mental health, family, or personal). They open up to you. The MOST supportive response is:

options.a: Share your own story of difficulty to show that you understand what they are facing.
options.b: Listen fully, validate their feelings, and ask what kind of support they would find useful.
options.c: Immediately offer advice on what they should do to get through what they are facing now.
options.d: Tell them they are strong and they will definitely get through this difficult period.

question_text: Your friend group expects you to be available every weekend, but you are exhausted and need recovery time. The MOST healthy response is:

options.a: Attend the plans but stay only briefly to fulfill the social obligation to your friends.
options.b: Be honest: "I need a quieter weekend. Let's plan something fun for the next one."
options.c: Force yourself to attend the plans to avoid disappointing the rest of the friend group.
options.d: Stop attending the weekend plans entirely without any explanation to the friend group.

question_text: For the past 3 weeks, you have felt persistently low, lost interest in things you usually enjoy, and have trouble sleeping. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Wait another month or so — these things often pass on their own without any action.
options.b: Talk to a trusted person and consider speaking with a mental health professional soon.
options.c: Watch motivational content online and try to stay positive throughout the difficult phase.
options.d: Take a break from college and travel somewhere to reset your mood and perspective.

question_text: You have been avoiding starting an important task for several days. Each day you feel worse about it. The MOST effective intervention is:

options.a: Wait until you feel motivated to start the task before attempting to begin work on it.
options.b: Promise yourself a large reward once the entire task is fully completed and submitted.
options.c: Block out a full day to do it from scratch in one focused session next week.
options.d: Break it into the smallest first step ("open the doc, write one sentence") and just do.

question_text: You constantly compare yourself unfavourably to peers on social media, and it is affecting your mood. The MOST psychologically healthy response is:

options.a: Try harder in your own life to match the visible success they appear to be enjoying online.
options.b: Post more of your own achievements yourself to compete with what your peers are sharing.
options.c: Recognise it's curated highlights, audit who you follow, mute triggers, set time limits.
options.d: Quit social media completely and indefinitely to remove the source of the comparison entirely.

question_text: Which data structure is most appropriate for implementing a browser's back-button feature?

options.a: Hash table — keyed lookup access.
options.b: Linked list — sequential access only.
options.c: Stack — last-in, first-out access.
options.d: Queue — first-in, first-out access.

question_text: In Git, what happens when you `git push` a branch that another developer has updated on the remote since your last pull?

options.a: The push overwrites the remote with your local changes.
options.b: The push is rejected and requires you to pull and merge.
options.c: The push completes and merges the changes automatically.
options.d: The push creates a new branch on the remote repository.

question_text: Which SQL statement correctly returns the count of unique countries in a Customers table?

options.a: SELECT COUNT(country) GROUP BY country FROM Customers;
options.b: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT country) FROM Customers;
options.c: SELECT DISTINCT COUNT(country) FROM Customers;
options.d: SELECT UNIQUE(country) FROM Customers;

question_text: Which HTTP status code indicates that the requested resource was not found on the server?
In object-oriented programming, 'polymorphism' refers to:

options.a: Objects of different types responding to the same interface.
options.b: Inheritance from a single parent across many child classes.
options.c: Multiple methods sharing the same name in a single class.
options.d: Hiding implementation details behind a public interface.

question_text: In a RESTful API, which HTTP method is conventionally used to UPDATE an existing resource?

options.a: DELETE method for removal.
options.b: POST method for creation.
options.c: PUT or PATCH HTTP method.
options.d: GET method for retrieval.

question_text: A 'NULL' value in a relational database represents:

options.a: The absence of a value, distinct from zero or empty.
options.b: A zero value for numeric columns across the table.
options.c: An empty string for textual columns across the table.
options.d: A default value assigned when no value is provided.

question_text: Which of the following best describes 'IaaS' (Infrastructure as a Service)?

options.a: Containerised applications that scale across data centres.
options.b: Virtualised compute, storage, and networking from a provider.
options.c: Software accessed over the web with no local install required.
options.d: A managed platform on which you deploy your own custom code.

question_text: What is 'phishing' in a cybersecurity context?

options.a: Encrypting a user's files and demanding payment for their release.
options.b: Injecting malicious code into a website's user input fields.
options.c: Tricking users into revealing credentials or transferring money.
options.d: Overwhelming a server with traffic to make the site unavailable.

question_text: What is the primary characteristic of a 'cryptographic hash function' like SHA-256?

options.a: Reversible: original input can be recovered from the hash.
options.b: One-way: easy to compute, infeasible to reverse.
options.c: Reproducible: produces different outputs each time it runs.
options.d: Variable: output length depends on the input data given.

question_text: Which sorting algorithm has an average-case time complexity of O(n²)?

options.a: Heap sort.
options.b: Merge sort.
options.c: Quick sort.
options.d: Bubble sort.

question_text: Which of the following is a key difference between TCP and UDP protocols?

options.a: TCP guarantees delivery and order; UDP does neither.
options.b: TCP is faster than UDP for media streaming use cases.
options.c: TCP is application-layer; UDP is transport-layer.
options.d: TCP is connectionless; UDP is connection-oriented.

question_text: What is the primary difference between a Docker container and a virtual machine (VM)?

options.a: Containers are stateless; VMs maintain persistent state by default.
options.b: Containers share the host kernel; VMs run a full guest OS.
options.c: Containers run on cloud servers; VMs run on local hardware.
options.d: Containers run one process; VMs run many processes at once.

question_text: What does 'CI/CD' stand for in modern software development?

options.a: Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery.
options.b: Code Integration / Code Deployment.
options.c: Container Integration / Container Distribution.
options.d: Cloud Integration / Cloud Deployment.

question_text: Two-factor authentication (2FA) typically combines:

options.a: Something you know with something you have or are.
options.b: A user identifier plus a secret password.
options.c: Two different passwords used during the login sequence.
options.d: A password plus a security question used as backup.

question_text: What does 'overfitting' mean in a machine learning context?

options.a: Model has more parameters than the available training data.
options.b: Model takes too long to converge on a training run.
options.c: Model fits the training data but fails on new data.
options.d: Model is trained on too large a dataset for its design.

question_text: What does an LLM (Large Language Model) like GPT or Claude fundamentally do at the token level?

options.a: Predict the next token based on the preceding tokens.
options.b: Match the user's input to the closest known answer.
options.c: Apply logical rules to interpret the user's question.
options.d: Retrieve relevant content from a stored knowledge base.

question_text: Which of the following best describes 'end-to-end encryption' (E2EE)?

options.a: Data is encrypted while moving between two servers.
options.b: Only sender and recipient can read the message content.
options.c: Data is encrypted on disk wherever it is stored.
options.d: The service provider holds keys to decrypt all messages.

question_text: In JavaScript, what is the primary purpose of 'async/await'?

options.a: Writing asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code.
options.b: Running multiple tasks in parallel across multiple CPU threads.
options.c: Avoiding callbacks entirely in modern JavaScript code patterns.
options.d: Delaying code execution until a specific clock time arrives.

question_text: What is an 'API key' typically used for?

options.a: Uniquely identifying a user across multiple services.
options.b: Authorising payment for usage of a service.
options.c: Authenticating and authorising calls to a service.
options.d: Encrypting requests sent to a third-party service.

question_text: What is the term for a function that calls itself, typically with a smaller input each time?

options.a: Branching.
options.b: Iteration.
options.c: Looping.
options.d: Recursion.

question_text: You've written code that has just been deployed to production. A user reports it is causing errors. What is the MOST appropriate first action?

options.a: Investigate the root cause fully and completely before taking any action on the issue.
options.b: Wait for more user reports to come in to confirm whether the issue is real or one-off.
options.c: Roll back to the previous version immediately without investigating what the actual issue is.
options.d: Verify the issue, assess severity and scope, then roll back, hotfix, or mitigate as needed.

question_text: A teammate has submitted code for review. You notice 5 issues — 3 are stylistic preferences, 2 are real bugs. The MOST effective review approach is:

options.a: Flag all 5 issues with equal emphasis to be thorough in your review of their work.
options.b: Let it go entirely — code reviews shouldn't be too picky about minor stylistic things.
options.c: Flag the 2 bugs as required changes and mention stylistic items as optional suggestions.
options.d: Flag only the 2 bugs and ignore the stylistic preferences since they are not real issues.

question_text: You are using an AI coding assistant (like Copilot or Claude) to write code for an assignment. The MOST professionally appropriate approach is:

options.a: Use AI freely but make small edits to the output before submitting the final assignment.
options.b: Use AI as an aid: review each line, ensure you understand, test it, disclose if required.
options.c: Refuse to use AI tools at all to ensure that all of your submitted work is original.
options.d: Submit the AI's output as your own work without any disclosure of where it came from.

question_text: Your team is moving to a new technology stack you don't know. You have one month before you'll be expected to contribute. The MOST effective learning approach is:

options.a: Take an expensive certification course to ensure your preparation is thorough and complete.
options.b: Wait until your team's projects start and learn the new stack on the job as you go.
options.c: Read every book and tutorial available on the stack before writing any of your own code.
options.d: Combine quick orientation with hands-on practice, build small projects, ask questions.

question_text: You discover a bug in an open-source library you use, and you have a working fix. The MOST professionally valuable action is:

options.a: Open an issue describing the bug, then submit a pull request following the project guide.
options.b: Fork the library and maintain your own version of it going forward for your own use.
options.c: Keep the fix to yourself and apply it locally — it's not your job to fix the library.
options.d: Email the maintainer of the project directly to share your fix with them privately.

question_text: You notice that a colleague's code is exposing API credentials in plain text on a public repository. The MOST appropriate immediate action is:

options.a: Email the team manager about the issue so the colleague is formally held responsible.
options.b: Make the repo private quietly without telling the colleague or anyone else about it.
options.c: Post a comment on the public repo identifying the exposed credentials so they see it.
options.d: Notify them privately, suggest rotating credentials, scrub git history, use secrets.

question_text: I am confident in my ability to learn a new programming language or framework when needed.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I enjoy exploring new technologies even when they are not directly required for my current work.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When my code breaks, I am methodical about isolating the cause rather than guessing randomly.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: When I encounter an unfamiliar tool or library, I am comfortable reading official documentation as a first step.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I use AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, etc.) thoughtfully in my work — verifying outputs rather than copy-pasting blindly.

options.a: Strongly Disagree
options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you work on personal coding/tech projects outside of class or work requirements?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you read technical blogs, papers, or release notes related to technologies you use?

options.a: Almost Never (0–10% of the time)
options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)
